inlines.text: [Updated on 2021-02-01: Updated to version 2.0 with several work added and many typos fixed.]
[Updated on 2021-05-26: Add P-tuning and Prompt Tuning in the

There is a gigantic amount of free text on the Web, several magnitude more than labelled benchmark datasets. The state-of-the-art language models (LM) are trained with unsupervised Web data in large scale. When generating samples from LM by iteratively sampling the next token, we do not have much control over attributes of the output text, such as the topic, the style, the sentiment, etc. Many applications would demand a good control over the model output. For example, if we plan to use LM to generate reading materials for kids, we would like to guide the output stories to be safe, educational and easily understood by children.

How to steer a powerful unconditioned language model? In this post, we will delve into several approaches for controlled content generation with an unconditioned langage model.
Note that model steerability is still an open research question. Each introduced method has certain pros & cons.
Apply guided decoding strategies and select desired outputs at test time.
Optimize for the most desired outcomes via good prompt design.
Fine-tune the base model or steerable layers to do conditioned content generation.
In the following discussion, we assume we have access to a pretrained generative language model
. The model has learned the distribution over token sequences by optimizing for the next token prediction:

By adopting different decoding methods, we can place restrictions or preferences on the sampling process to alter the generated samples without modifying any model weights. Even though decoding strategies do not change the values of any trainable parameter, it is a quite important component.
Since the final layer of the model predicts logits
over the vocabulary space, the next token can be sampled by applying softmax with temperature
A low temperature would make the distribution sharper and a high value makes it softer.
: Always pick the next token with the
. However, it tends to create repetitions of phrases, even for well-trained models.
: It essentially does breadth-first search, one token per tree level, but with a limited bandwidth. At each level of the search tree, beam search keeps track of
(named “beam width”) best candidates and expands all the successors of these candidates in the next level. Beam search could stop expanding a node if it hits the EOS (end-of-sentence) token.
However, maximization-based decoding does not guarantee high-quality generation.

attrs.alt: The probability assigned to the next token by beam search versus by humans. The human selected tokens have much higher variance in predicted probability and thus more surprising. (Image source: Holtzman et al. 2019 )

inlines.text: ): At each sampling step, only the top
most likely tokens are selected and the probability mass is redistributed among them. In
where the next token is randomly selected among the top
most likely candidates and they argued that this approach can generate more novel and less repetitive content than beam search.
): Also known as “Top-p sampling”. One drawback of top-k sampling is that the predefined number
does not take into consideration how

the probability distribution might be. The nucleus sampling selects the smallest set of top candidates with the cumulative probability exceeding a threshold (e.g. 0.95) and then the distribution is rescaled among selected candidates.
Both top-k and nucleus sampling have less repetitions with a proper set of hyperparameters.
): To avoid the common failure case of generating duplicate substrings, the

paper proposed a new sampling method to penalize repetitions by discounting the scores of previously generated tokens. The probability distribution for the next token with repetition penalty is defined as:

attrs.latex:
p_i = \frac{\exp(o_i / (T \cdot \mathbb{1}(i \in g)))}{\sum_j \exp(o_j / (T \cdot \mathbb{1}(j \in g)))} \quad
\mathbb{1}(c) = \theta \text{ if the condition }c\text{ is True else }1

inlines.text: contains a set of previously generated tokens,
is found to yield a good balance between less repetition and truthful generation.

All the above standard decoding strategies sample tokens according to the predicted probability, with no additional information. Our preferences on topic or sentiment can be baked into the candidate ranking function to guide the sample generation by altering the candidate ranking score. The ranking score for token selection at each decoding step can be set as a combination of LM log-likelihood and a set of desired feature discriminators. The features are designed to quantify human preferences by heuristics (
built a system called “Hafez” for generating poetry in desired style by adjusting sampling weights in beam search at decoding steps. The likelihood of sampling for the next token
is augmented by a scoring function:
is the log-likelihood predicted by LM.
is the accumulated score of the already-generated words in the current beam state
. The green part can incorporate many different features for steering the style of the output. A set of feature functions
define the preferences and the associated weights
work like “control knobs” that can be easily customized at decoding time. Features can measure a variety of attributes and can be easily combined; for example,
exists in a bag of desired or banned topical words.
needs to take the history as input too).
if longer or shorter words are in particular preferred.
manually designed features for ranking and altered the sampling distribution by appending similarity scores between topic distribution or embeddings of the context and the completion.
adopted a set of learned discriminators, each specializing in a different principle of communication guided by

: quality, quantity, relation and manner. The discriminators learn to encode these desired principles by measuring repetition, entailment, relevance, and lexical diversity, respectively. Given some ground truth completion, all the discriminator models are trained to minimize the ranking log-likelihood,
is expected to obtain a higher score than the generated one
are also learned to minimize the score difference between the golden standard and the generated completion.  Discriminative Adversarial Search (DAS;

) is inspired by GAN and trains the discriminator to tell apart human created text from machine generated text. The discriminator predicts a label for each token instead of for the entire sequence. The discriminator logprob is added to the score to guide sampling towards the human-written style.
studied beam search in a regularized decoding framework:
Since we expect maximum probability to have minimum surprise, the surprisal of a LM at time step

The MAP (maximum a posteriori) part demands for sequences with maximum probability given context, while the regularizer introduces other constraints. It is possible a global optimal strategy may need to have a high-surprisal step occasionally so that it can shorten the output length or produce more low-surprisal steps afterwards.
Beam search has gone through the test of time in the field of NLP. The question is:

children.text: If we want to model beam search as exact search in a regularized decoding framework, how should

inlines.text: The paper proposed a connection between beam search and the

“The uniform information density hypothesis (UID; Levy and Jaeger, 2007)  states that—subject to the constraints of the grammar—humans prefer sentences that distribute information (in the sense of information theory) equally across the linguistic signal, e.g., a sentence.”

In other words, it hypothesizes that humans prefer text with evenly distributed surprisal. Popular decoding methods like top-k sampling or nuclear sampling actually filter out high-surprisal options, thus implicitly encouraging the UID property in output sequences.
The paper experimented with several forms of regularizers:
, we have greedy search. Note that being greedy at each individual step does not guarantee global optimality.
is the average surprisal over all timesteps. It directly encodes the UID hypothesis.
; this decoding regularizer encourages adjacent tokens to have similar surprisal.
penalizes the maximum compensation of surprisal.
encourages all the tokens to have surprisal close to 0.
An experiment with greedy regularizers showed that larger
results in better performance (e.g. measured by BLEU for NMT task) and lower std dev of surprisal.

attrs.alt: The plot of BLEU and std. dev of surprisals as functions of the strength of the regularizer $\lambda$. The subgraph in grey shows the relationship between BLEU and surprisal std. dev. (Image source: Meister et al. 2020 )

inlines.text: A default beam search would have text generation of decreased quality when beam size increases. Regularized beam search greatly helps alleviate this issue. A combined regularizer further improves the performance. In their experiments for NMT, they found
for squared work out as the optimal combined regularizer.

attrs.alt: The plot of BLEU of a function of beam size (left) and BLEU scores for translations created by different regularized decoding strategies. (Image source: Meister et al. 2020 )

inlines.text: Guided decoding essentially runs a more expensive beam search where the sampling probability distribution is altered by side information about human preferences.
algorithm to maximize an arbitrary objective for sampling sequences. The idea is based on the

). NPAD injects unstructured noise into the model hidden states and runs noisy decoding multiple times in parallel to avoid potential degradation. To take a step further, trainable greedy decoding replaces the unstructured noise with a learnable random variable, predicted by a RL agent that takes the previous hidden state, the previous decoded token and the context as input. In other words, the decoding algorithm learns a RL actor to manipulate the model hidden states for better outcomes.
trained a binary classifier to distinguish samples from data distribution and samples from the generative model. This classifier is used to estimate
for constructing a new unnormalized distribution. The proposed strategy is called
be the real data distribution and
be a learned generative model. A classical approach for evaluating the expectation of a given function
can only be estimated via finite datasets. Let
be a probabilistic binary classifier for predicting whether a sample
is from the true data distribution (
, the importance weight can be estimated by:
Since we cannot learn a perfect optimal classifier, the importance weight would be an estimation
. A couple of practical tricks can be applied to offset cases when the classifier exploits artifacts in the generated samples to make very confident predictions (i.e. very small importance weights):
Self-normalization: normalize the weight by the sum
Flattening: add a power scaling parameter
To sample from an importance resampled generative model,

The goal is to learn the parameters of the energy function
gets closer to the desired data distribution. The residual energy function is trained by noise contrastive estimation (

However, the partition function is intractable in practice. The paper proposed a simple way to first sample from the original LM and then to resample from them according to the energy function. This is unfortunately quite expensive.

attrs.alt: Top k samples from the base LM are resampled according to the residual energy function. (Image source: Deng et al., 2020 )

inlines.text: Large language models have been shown to be very powerful on many NLP tasks, even with only

. The prompt design has a big impact on the performance on downstream tasks and often requires time-consuming manual crafting. For example, factual questions can gain a big boost with smart prompt design in “closed-book exam” (
). I’m expecting to see an increasing amount of literature on automatic smart prompt design.
) is a method to automatically create prompts for various tasks via gradient-based search. AutoPrompt constructs a prompt by combining the original task inputs
with a collection of trigger tokens
. The trigger tokens are shared across all inputs and thus

attrs.alt: The overview of AutoPrompt. The trigger tokens are retrieved to optimize for the target outputs across all inputs. (Image source: Shin et al., 2020 )

inlines.text: The universal trigger tokens are identified using a gradient-guided search strategy same as in
setting means that the trigger tokens
can optimize for the target output
for all inputs from a dataset:
The search operates in the embedding space. The embedding of every trigger token
is first initialized to some default value and then gets updated to minimize the first-order Taylor expansion of the task-specific loss around the current token embedding:
refers to the embedding matrix of all the tokens.
is the average gradient of the task loss over a batch at iteration
-dimensional dot product, which is cheap and can be computed in parallel.

attrs.alt: We search for trigger tokens by updating their embeddings with the gradient of the task loss per batch. (Image source: Wallace et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: The above token replacement method can be augmented with beam search. When looking for the optimal token embedding
candidates instead of a single one, searching from left to right and score each beam by

attrs.alt: Example prompts discovered by AutoPrompt for different tasks. (Image source: Shin et al., 2020 )

inlines.text: Smart prompt design essentially produces efficient context that can lead to desired completion. Motivated by this observation,

The performance increases with the prefix length

attrs.alt: Task performance, summarization (left) and table-to-text (right), as a function of prefix length. (Image source: Li & Liang 2021 )

inlines.text: A few other interesting learnings from their ablation studies include:
Tuning only the embedding layer (without prefix) is not sufficiently expressive.
, slightly underperforms prefix-tuning, likely because it only affects the context for
leads to low performance with high variance. In contrast, initializing
with activations of real words improves generation, even the words are irrelevant to the task.

Fine-tuned models achieve better task performance but they can fail in the low data regime. Both AutoPrompt and Prefix-Tuning were found to outperform fine-tuning in the regime where the training dataset is small (i.e.

samples). As an alternative to fine-tuning, prompt design or learning the context embedding is much cheaper. AutoPrompt improves the accuracy for sentiment classification a lot more than manual prompts and achieves similar performance as linear probing. For the NLI task, AutoPrompt obtains higher accuracy than linear probing. It is able to retrieve facts more accurately than manual prompts too. In low data regime, Prefix-Tuning achieves performance comparable with fine-tuning on table-to-text generation and summarization.

), follow the similar idea of explicit training continuous prompt embeddings but with a few different choices over the trainable parameters and architecture. Different from Prefix-Tuning which concatenates continuous prompt tokens in every hidden state layer of the transformer, both P-tuning and Prompt Tuning non-invasively add continuous prompts

looks like the following and the pseudo-token hidden state can be optimized with gradient descent.

attrs.alt: The illustration of P-tuning. Sometimes, adding a few task-related anchor tokens, such as “capital” in the figure, can bring further improvement. (Image source: Liu et al. 2021 )

inlines.text: There are two major optimization challenges in P-tuning:
Discreteness: The word embedding of a pretrained language model are highly discrete. It is hard to optimize
if they are intialized at random.
should be dependent on each other. Thus they develop a mechanism to model this dependency by training a light-weighted LSTM-based prompt encoder:

P-tuning is more flexible than prefix-tuning, as it inserts trainable tokens in the middle of a prompt not just at the beginning. The usage of task-specific anchor tokens is like combining manual prompt engineering with trainable prompts.
) largely simplifies the idea of prefix tuning by only allowing an additional
tunable tokens per downstream task to be prepended to the input text. The conditional generation is
are embedding vectors and we have
Prompt tuning produces competitive results as model fine-tuning when the model gets
(billions of parameters and up). This result is especially interesting given that large models are expensive to fine-tune and execute at inference time.
With learned task-specific parameters, prompt tuning achieves better transfer learning when adapting to new domains. It outperforms fine-tuning on domain shift problems.
They also showed that prompt ensembling of multiple prompts for the same task introduces further improvement.

attrs.alt: The illustration of how Prompt Tuning works. (Image source: Lester et al. 2021 )

inlines.text: The experiments investigated several prompt initialization schemes:
Random initialization by uniformly sampling from [-0.5, 0.5];
Sample embeddings of top 5000 common tokens;

Use the embedding values of the class label strings. If we don’t have enough class labels to initialize the soft-prompt, we fall back to scheme 2.
Random initialization performs noticeably worse than the other two options.

attrs.alt: The effect of (a) different prompt initialization schemes and (b) different prompt lengths. (Image source: Lester et al. 2021 )

inlines.text: The pre-training objectives also have a big impact on the quality of prompt tuning. T5’s “span corruption” is not a good option here.

Prompt tuning is found to be less likely to overfit to a specific dataset. To evaluate the robustness to data shifting problem, they trained the model on one dataset of one task and evaluated it on the test dataset but in a
. Prompt tuning is more resilient and can generalize to different domains better.

attrs.alt: Prompt tuning is more resilient to domain shift between train and test sets. (Image source: Lester et al. 2021 )

inlines.text: Paraphrasing is a quick way to explore more prompts similar to the known version, which can be done via
.  Using back-translation, the initial prompt is translated into
candidates in another language and then each is translated back into
candidates in the original language. The resulting total
candidates are scored and ranked by their round-trip probabilities.
by generating a variety of paraphrases
until it triggers a different prediction of target function
into multiple languages and then translating it back to the original language.
), etc. They are considered as “bugs” in the model. Applying those rules as data augmentation in model training helps robustify the model and fix bugs.

attempts to validate whether a trained language model knows certain knowledge by automatically discovering better prompts to query. Within the scope of knowledge retrieval where factual knowledge is represented in the form of a triple
(subject, relation, object). The prompts can be mined from training sentences (e.g. Wikipedia description) or expanded by paraphrase.
Interestingly some small modifications in the prompts may lead to big gain, as shown in Fig. X.

attrs.alt: Small modifications in prompt templates can lead to big performance gains: replacement in blue, insertion in green, deletion in red. (Image source: Jiang et al., 2020 )

inlines.text: Fine-tuning is an intuitive way to guide a LM to output desired content, commonly by training on supervised datasets or by RL. We can fine-tune all the weights in the model or restrict the fine-tuning to only top or additional layers.
Conditional training aims to learn a generative model conditioned on a control variable

trained a conditional language model for 2-step story generation. First, a model outputs the story sketch and then a story writing model creates a story following that sketch. The mechanism of conditioning on the sketch is implemented by a
model architecture. The fusion model enforces a form of
that allows the story writing model to focus on learning what the first sketch generation model is missing. Also for story generation,
experimented with an ending valence-conditioned story generator LM,
is the label of the story ending (sad, happy or neutral). Their language model is a bidirectional LSTM and the label is mapped into a learned embedding which then blends into the LSTM cell.
) aims to train a language model conditioned control code
using controllable datasets. CTRL learns the conditioned distribution
by training on raw text sequences with

, etc. Then the learned model is able to generate text with respect to the prompt prefix. The training data contains Wikipedia, OpenWebText, books, Amazon reviews, reddit corpus and many more, where each dataset is assigned with a control code and subreddit in the reddit corpus has its own topic as control code.

attrs.alt: Datasets used for training CTRL and associated control codes. (Image source: Edited from Table 7 in Keskar et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: The control code also can be used for
, assuming the prior over domains is uniform. One limitation of CTRL is the lack of control for

attrs.alt: The examples of conditioned sample generation by CTRL. (Image source: Keskar et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: Note that CTRL trains a transformer model from scratch. However, labelling all the text within the same dataset with the same control code (e.g. All the wikipedia articles have “wikipedia” as control code) feels quite constrained. Considering that often we need highly customized control codes but only have a limited amount of labelled data, I would expect fine-tuning an unconditional LM with a small labelled dataset in the same way as CTRL to work out well too. Although how much data is needed and how good the sample quality might be are subject to experimentation.
Fine-tuning a sequential model with RL regarding any arbitrary and possibly non-differentiable reward function has been proved to work well years ago (
). RL fine-tuning can resolve several problems with

method. With teacher forcing, the model only minimizes a maximum-likelihood loss at each individual decoding step during training but it is asked to predict the entire sequence from scratch at test time. Such a discrepancy between train and test could lead to exposure bias and accumulated error. In contrast, RL fine-tuning is able to directly optimize task-specific metrics on the sequence level, such as BLEU for translation (
) and customized metric for story generation (

applied REINFORCE to train RNN models for sequence generation tasks. The model is first trained to predict the next token using cross-entropy loss (ML loss) and then fine-tuned alternatively by both ML loss and REINFORCE (RL loss). At the second fine-tuning stage, the number of training steps for next-token prediction is gradually decreasing until none and eventually only RL loss is used. This sequence-level RL fine-tuning was shown by experiments to lead to great improvements over several supervised learning baselines back then.
Google implemented the similar approach in their neural machine translation system (
adopted such approach for summarization task. The training objective contains two parts, ML loss for next token prediction,
for maximizing the expected reward where the reward per sequence is measured by BLEU or ROUGE. The model is first trained with
until convergence and then fine-tuned with a linear combination of two losses,
The RL loss of Google NMT is to maximize the expected BLEU score:
added an extra weighting term based on the reward difference between two output sequences,
by sampling the next token according to the predicted probability and
by greedily taking the most likely token. This RL loss maximizes the conditional likelihood of the sampled sequence
if it obtains a higher reward than the greedy baseline

attrs.latex: \mathcal{L}_\text{RL} = \sum_{(x, y^*)\sim\mathcal{D}} (R(\hat{y}, y^*) - R(y, y^*)) \sum_{t=1}^{n'} \log p(y_t \vert y_{<{t}}, x)

inlines.text: Reward learning is critical for defining human preferences. Quantitative measurement like BLEU or ROUGE computes the overlap of words and n-gram phrases between sequences and does not always correlate with better quality by human judges. Reward learning from human feedback (
) is a better way to align what we measure with what we actually care about. Human feedback has been applied to learn a reward function for applications like story generation (
In order to generate more coherent conversation,

collected 4 types of binary human feedback given a conversation pair (user utterance, system response), whether the system response is (1) comprehensive, (2) on topic, (3) interesting and (4) leading to continuation of the conversation.
An evaluator is trained to predict human feedback and then is used to rerank the beam search samples, to finetune the model or to do both. (Actually they didn’t use RL fine-tuning but rather use the evaluator to provide a discriminator loss in supervised fine-tuning.)
Let’s define a learned reward function
as a measurement for the quality of output
To learn the ground truth reward
(1) Regression loss: simply minimizing the mean squared error.
(2) Preference loss: learning to agree with the ground truth reward,
achieves the best performance, where the reward model is a thin MLP layer on top of BERT sentence embedding.
collected human labels by asking humans to select the best candidate
y_0, y_1 \sim p(.\vert x), y_2, y_3 \sim \pi(.\vert x)
. We should be aware that human labeling might have very high disagreement when the ground truth is fuzzy.

attrs.alt: The overview of the training framework for fine-tuning a language model policy with reward learned from human feedback. (Image source: Ziegler et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: The reward model is implemented by a pretrained language model with an extra random linear layer of the final embedding output. It it trained to minimize the loss:
To keep the scale consistent during training, the reward model is normalized to have mean 0 and variance 1.
, initialized by a pretrained language model
with the above learned reward model. To avoid the policy’s deviating from its original behavior too much, a

attrs.latex: R(x, y) = R_\psi(x, y) - \beta\log\frac{\pi(y \vert x)}{p(y \vert x)}

inlines.text: If running online data collection, human label collection process is continued during RL fine-tuning and thus the human labelers can review results generated by the latest policy. The number of human labels are evenly spread out during the training process. Meanwhile the reward model is also retrained periodically. Online data collection turns out to be important for the summarization task but not for the text continuation task. In their experiments, jointly training the reward model and the policy with shared parameters did not work well and can lead to overfitting due to the big imbalance between dataset sizes.
), the human label collection was further simplified to select the best option between a pair of summaries,
The reward model loss was updated to optimize the log odds of the selected summary:

attrs.latex: \mathcal{L}_\text{rm} = - \mathbb{E}_{(x, y_0, y_1, b)\sim\mathcal{D}} [\log(\sigma(r_\theta(x, y_b) − r_\theta(x, y_{1−b})))]

attrs.alt: The overview of fine-tuning the language model policy from human feedback for summarization, including (1) human feedback collection, (2) reward model training, and (3) policy training. (Image source: Stiennon et al., 2020 )

inlines.text: Instead of fine-tuning the entire model, only fine-tuning a small extra set of parameters while the base model stays fixed is computationally cheaper.
In computer vision, plug-and-play generative networks (PPGN;
) generate images with different attributes by plugging a discriminator
. Then the sample with a desired attribute
) combines one or multiple simple attribute models with a pretrained language model for controllable text generation.
. To control content generation, the current latent representation at time
(containing a list of key-value pairs per layer), can be shifted by
in the direction of the sum of two gradients:
One toward higher log-likelihood of the attribute
— so that the output content acquires a desired attribute.
The other toward higher log-likelihood of the unmodified language model
— so that the generated text is still in fluent and smooth natural language.
To shift the output, at decoding time, PPLM runs one forward → one backward → one forward, three passes in total:
First a forward pass is performed to compute the likelihood of attribute
be a stepwise update to the hidden state
shifts the distribution of generated text closer to having the attribute

is initialized at zero.
Then a backward pass updates the LM hidden states using normalized gradients from the attribute model

attrs.latex: \Delta H_t \leftarrow \Delta H_t + \alpha \frac{\nabla_{\Delta H_t} \log p(a|H_t + \Delta H_t)}{\| \nabla_{\Delta H_t} \log p(a|H_t + \Delta H_t) \|^\gamma}

inlines.text: is a normalization scaling coefficient, set per layer.
is step size. This update can be repeated

times
3. The final forward pass recomputes a new distribution over the vocabulary, generated from the updated latents
. The next token is sampled from the updated distribution.

attrs.alt: The overview of how PPLM runs three passes to update the model output to increase the likelihood of a desired attribute. (Image source: Dathathri et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: Multiple attribute models can be mix-and-matched during generation with customized weights, acting as a set of “control knobs”. The PPLM paper explored two types of attribute models:
The simplest attribution model is based on a predefined
, that specifies a topic of interest.
To encourage the model to output the desired words at least once but not at every step, they normalize the gradient by the maximum gradient norm.
Interestingly, they found that increasing the probability of generating words in the bag also increases the probability of generating

but not identical words about the same topic.
2. The discriminator attribute models are based on learned classifiers which define preferences by a distribution instead of hard samples.
To ensure the fluency in language, PPLM applied two additional designs:
Minimizing the KL diverge between modified and unmodified LM, commonly seen in other RL fine-tuning approaches (see
to constantly tie the generated text to the unconditional LM
are the unmodified and modified output distributions, respectively.
balances between prediction from before and after models.

attrs.alt: Examples of controllable text generation by PPLM. (Image source: Dathathri et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: Interestingly, they found a large variance in the extent of controllability across topics. Some topics (religion, science, politics) are easier to control for compared to others (computers, space).
One obvious drawback of PPLM is that due to multiple passes at every decoding step, the test time computation becomes much more expensive.
) incorporates the future context by back-propagation. Given input text
, DELOREAN aims to generate continuation completion
satisfies certain constraints defined by a context

Then linearly combine two logits together to create a new representation

) trains a light-weighted side network that learns a residual on top of the original model outputs without modifying the pre-trained model weights. Unlike PPLM, no gradient update is applied on the hidden states. It is a simple yet effective approach for incremental learning. The base model is treated as a black-box model and does not necessarily have to be a neural network. Side-tuning setup assumes the base and side models are fed exactly the same input and the side model is independently learned.

attrs.alt: Comparison of fixed weights, fine-tuning and side-tuning. (Image source: Zhang et al., 2019 )

inlines.text: The paper explored different strategies of fusing predictions from the base and side models:
are comparable. Side-tuning is able to achieve better performance, when it is trained with intermediate amounts of data and when the base network is large.
) supplements the original pre-trained model with an
model that shifts the output distribution according to the target task. The base and auxiliary model outputs are merged on the logits level. The combined model is trained to maximize the likelihood
can be decomposed into two parts:
assigns high probabilities to fluent sequences of tokens;

p(x_t\vert x_{<{t}}, z)
= \frac{p(z \vert x_{\leq t})}{p(z)} p(x_t \vert x_{<{t}}) 
\propto p(z \vert x_{\leq t}) p(x_t \vert x_{<{t}})

inlines.text: They finetuned a GPT2-medium model with control code similar to how

is trained to form a CC-LM using a linear combination of discriminative loss and generative loss. This discriminator model is then used as GiDe to guide generation by a larger language model like GPT2-XL.
One way of decoding from GeDi is to sample from a weighted posterior
p^w(x_{t+1}\vert x_{1:t}, z) \propto p(z \vert x_{1:t+1})^w p(x_{t+1} \vert x_{1:t})
applies additional bias toward the desired class
. In the sampling process, only tokens with the class or next-token probability larger than a certain threshold are selected.
GeDi guided generation in their experiments showed strong controllability and ran 30x faster than
) frames controlled text generation as the optimization of a probability distribution with a constraint. It involves two major steps.

attrs.alt: The algorithm of distributional policy gradient to make it possible to sample from a EBM $P(x)$, where $q$ is initialized to be $a$. (Image source: Khalifa, et al. 2020 )

inlines.text: This approach can be used to model various constraints in controllable text generation:
is a binary feature; such as constraining the presence or absence of words, or classifier-based constraints.

represents a probability distribution; such as constraining the probability of gender, topic, etc. Their experiments showed great progress in debiasing a GPT-2 model that was trained on Wikipedia Biographies corpus. The percentage of generated biographies on females increased from 7.4% to 35.6%.
Hybrid constraints: combine multiple constraints by simply summing them up.

attrs.alt: Debiasing experiments using GDC with various constraints. (Image source: Khalifa, et al. 2020 )

inlines.text: Compared to other baselines, GDC using pointwise constraints diverges less from the base model

attrs.alt: Compare pointwise constrained GDC with several baselines. Low Self-BLEU-5 and high Dist-1 indicate high diversity. (Image source: Khalifa, et al. 2020 )

inlines.text: in Fig. X.) without constraints converges fast but has a high deviation from the original model.
in Fig. X.) has low sample diversity.
GDC has smoother learning curves and produces a richer vocabulary.
The standard way of maximizing the log-likelihood loss in language model training leads to

, which cannot be fixed with only smart decoding methods. Such models tend to output high-frequency words too often and low-frequency words too rarely, especially when using deterministic decoding (e.g. greedy, beam search). In other words, they are overconfident in their predictions.
] tries to combat this and incorporates preference to
content into the training objective directly. It combines two updates:
A routine maximized likelihood update to assign true tokens with high probability;
A new type of unlikelihood update to avoid unwanted tokens with high probability.
and a set of negative candidate tokens
is to randomly select candidates from model-generated sequences.
The unlikelihood training can be extended to be on the

-level, where the negative continuation is defined by a sequence of per-step negative candidate sets. They should be designed to penalize properties that we don’t like. For example, we can penalize repeating n-grams as follows:

attrs.latex: \mathcal{C}^t_\text{repeat-n} = \{x_t\} \text{ if }(x_{t-i}, \dots, x_{t+j}) \in x_{<{t-i}} \text{ for any } (j-i)=n, i\leq n \leq j.

inlines.text: Their experiments used unlikelihood training to avoid repetitions in language model outputs and indeed showed better results on less repetition and more unique tokens compared to standard MLE training.
Weng, Lilian. (Jan 2021). Controllable neural text generation. Lil’Log. https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-01-02-controllable-text-generation/.

assets.alt: The probability assigned to the next token by beam search versus by humans. The human selected tokens have much higher variance in predicted probability and thus more surprising. (Image source: Holtzman et al. 2019 )

The plot of BLEU and std. dev of surprisals as functions of the strength of the regularizer $\lambda$. The subgraph in grey shows the relationship between BLEU and surprisal std. dev. (Image source: Meister et al. 2020 )
The plot of BLEU of a function of beam size (left) and BLEU scores for translations created by different regularized decoding strategies. (Image source: Meister et al. 2020 )
The algorithm for sampling from a generative model according to importance weights $\hat{w}(\mathbf{x}\_i)$ using SIR. (Image source: Grover et al., 2019) )
Top k samples from the base LM are resampled according to the residual energy function. (Image source: Deng et al., 2020 )
The overview of AutoPrompt. The trigger tokens are retrieved to optimize for the target outputs across all inputs. (Image source: Shin et al., 2020 )
We search for trigger tokens by updating their embeddings with the gradient of the task loss per batch. (Image source: Wallace et al., 2019 )
Example prompts discovered by AutoPrompt for different tasks. (Image source: Shin et al., 2020 )
Illustrations of fine-tuning versus prefix-tuning. (Image source: Li & Liang 2021 )
Task performance, summarization (left) and table-to-text (right), as a function of prefix length. (Image source: Li & Liang 2021 )
The illustration of P-tuning. Sometimes, adding a few task-related anchor tokens, such as “capital” in the figure, can bring further improvement. (Image source: Liu et al. 2021 )
The illustration of how Prompt Tuning works. (Image source: Lester et al. 2021 )
The effect of (a) different prompt initialization schemes and (b) different prompt lengths. (Image source: Lester et al. 2021 )
Prompt tuning is more resilient to domain shift between train and test sets. (Image source: Lester et al. 2021 )
Small modifications in prompt templates can lead to big performance gains: replacement in blue, insertion in green, deletion in red. (Image source: Jiang et al., 2020 )
Datasets used for training CTRL and associated control codes. (Image source: Edited from Table 7 in Keskar et al., 2019 )
The examples of conditioned sample generation by CTRL. (Image source: Keskar et al., 2019 )
The overview of the training framework for fine-tuning a language model policy with reward learned from human feedback. (Image source: Ziegler et al., 2019 )

The overview of fine-tuning the language model policy from human feedback for summarization, including (1) human feedback collection, (2) reward model training, and (3) policy training. (Image source: Stiennon et al., 2020 )
The overview of how PPLM runs three passes to update the model output to increase the likelihood of a desired attribute. (Image source: Dathathri et al., 2019 )
Examples of controllable text generation by PPLM. (Image source: Dathathri et al., 2019 )
Comparison of fixed weights, fine-tuning and side-tuning. (Image source: Zhang et al., 2019 )
The auxiliary model is trained by reusing features extracted from multiple layers of the base model. (Image source: Zeldes et al., 2020 )
